claims.text: Ancient peoples — including Homeric Greeks and the authors of the Quran, Hebrew Bible, and ancient Chinese, Hindu, and Icelandic texts — never used the word 'blue' to describe anything, because their languages had not yet categorized blue as a distinct color.

evidence.quote:
these ancient texts
reference colors with pretty much the
same proportion as homer's epics

claims.rationale: Linguistic-historical consensus following Lazarus Geiger, Guy Deutscher (Through the Language Glass), and the Berlin & Kay typology: across independent ancient text corpora, no color term unambiguously denoting blue appears. The video's claim generalizes a well-attested finding about Homer and extends it cross-culturally. Some exceptions exist (e.g., Egyptian hieroglyphics) which the video separately addresses in the Egyptian-dye segment.

claims.text: William Gladstone, the four-time British Prime Minister, noticed while reading Homer's Odyssey that Homer described the sea as 'wine dark' rather than blue or green, and became fascinated with Homer's odd color vocabulary.

evidence.quote:
gladstone was reading the odyssey and
noticed homer described the wine dark
sea not blue not green wine dark

claims.rationale: Historically documented. Gladstone's 1858 'Studies on Homer and the Homeric Age' (vol. III) contains the color-analysis chapter; he did serve four non-consecutive terms as UK PM (1868-74, 1880-85, 1886, 1892-94). Both facts are uncontroversial.

claims.text: In Homer's writing, 'blue' is referenced zero times, while black appears ~200, white ~100, red 13, and yellow and green fewer than 10 — a lexical absence that Gladstone could not explain except by conjecturing Homer was colorblind.

evidence.quote:
glad still wondered if
homer was colorblind and saw blue as
maybe something else so he went through
thousands of pages of homer's writing
and counted his references to color
black is mentioned almost 200 times
white about a hundred times red is
mentioned 13 times yellow and green
fewer than 10 times but blue
zero

claims.rationale: Gladstone's count ratios are broadly consistent with what philologists find in the Homeric corpus, though modern scholarship (e.g., Maria Michela Sassi) argues the absence is better explained as a conceptual/categorical difference than colorblindness. The arithmetic tallies are approximately correct; the 'colorblind' inference is the historically discarded hypothesis.

claims.text: Across languages, color-term vocabulary emerges in a nearly universal order: first black and white, then red, then yellow or green, and blue appears last in every major language.

evidence.quote:
but almost universally names for colors
emerge in the same order first a
language creates the words for black and
white the next color is always red after
red it's either yellow or green the last
color to appear in every major language
is blue

claims.rationale: This is essentially the Berlin & Kay (1969) 'Basic Color Terms' typology, which posits a partially-ordered evolutionary sequence across languages. It has been criticized and refined (the original stages are not strictly linear and there are counter-examples), but the generalization that 'blue' emerges late is well-supported.

claims.text: Blue is almost non-existent as a biological pigment: only ~10% of flowers are blue, and no bird, mammal, or reptile produces blue pigment — apparent blues in peacocks, blue jays, and blue-eyed humans come from microscopic light-scattering structures.

evidence.quote:
only 10 of flowers are
blue so pretty rare but even more rare
in animals birds mammals reptiles none
of them produce blue pigment

claims.rationale: Biologically well-documented. Structural color (thin-film interference in feathers, tapetum/iris stroma scattering) produces the blues of peacocks, blue jays, and blue eyes — actual blue pigment is extremely rare in animals. Olavarria-Gallegos and vision-ecology literature confirm.

claims.text: Blue finally emerged as a distinct color category when the Egyptians invented a way to produce blue dye, after which languages evolved to accommodate the new color.

evidence.quote:
finally emerges as its own color when
the egyptians invented a way to produce
a blue dye

claims.rationale: Egyptian blue (calcium copper silicate, CaCuSi4O10) is the first known synthetic pigment, produced c. 3200 BCE. The causal claim that this specifically triggered global lexical adoption of 'blue' is a just-so story — the pigment predates Homer by millennia, yet Homer (9th-8th c. BCE Greek) still lacked a blue term. The invention unquestionably happened; the direct causation to every language's lexicon is oversimplified.

claims.text: In Jules Davidoff's experiment, members of the Himba tribe in Africa — whose language has no word for blue — had measurably longer reaction times picking a blue square from 11 green ones than English-speakers do.

evidence.quote:
jules davidoff conducted an
experiment with the himba tribe in
africa and like ancient cultures the
himba have no word for blue to the himba
blue is just a shade of green so himba
participants were shown 12 squares on a
screen 11 of the squares were green one
was blue can you pick out the blue
square now of course you can you found
it immediately but the himba had trouble

claims.rationale: Davidoff, Davies & Roberson (2008) 'Color categories: Evidence for the cultural relativity hypothesis' reports reaction-time differences for Himba vs. English speakers on blue/green discrimination. The finding is real but widely sensationalized in pop science; subsequent replications show the effect is smaller than media reports suggest.

claims.text: The popular claim — repeated by the BBC and Business Insider — that the Himba could not see the blue square at all is false; the host states it is a complete fabrication and that the Himba merely had slower reaction times.

evidence.quote:
the bbc and business
insider made that part up it's a
complete fabrication

claims.rationale: [debunks] The host is correct that many pop-science retellings exaggerated Davidoff's original finding. The actual paper reports reaction-time differences, not inability to perceive. Treating the stronger claim as a media 'fabrication' is rhetorically sharp but factually supported by comparing the original paper to the BBC/Business Insider coverage.

claims.text: The Himba can distinguish subtle shades of green — which most English-speakers cannot — because their language has unique words for many green categories, priming perceptual discrimination.

evidence.quote:
the himba they saw
this different green as easily as we
found the blue square

claims.rationale: Also from the Davidoff et al. work: the inverse experiment (picking an odd green from 11 green squares) shows Himba outperforming English-speakers. Interpreted as evidence for linguistic-relativity-style priming. Robust but not an absolute, and the cognitive mechanism is debated.

claims.text: Isaac Newton's choice of seven colors for the visible spectrum was not scientifically derived but rather a numerological fit to the seven musical tones and Pythagoras's mystical preference for seven.

evidence.quote:
he initially thought there
were 11 unique colors but then he
thought maybe just five but a week has
seven days and there are seven tonal
notes in a musical octave and pythagoras
thought seven was a magical number so
newton said effort let's call it seven
colors

claims.rationale: Well-documented in the history of optics. Newton's 1704 'Opticks' divides the spectrum into seven bands explicitly to parallel the diatonic scale. Indigo was the late addition to reach seven. Philip Steadman and Mark Blayney have written on the numerological reasoning.

claims.text: Human vision is trichromatic — three cone types for red, green, and blue — allowing roughly one million distinguishable colors.

evidence.quote:
human vision is trichromatic this means
we have three different photoreceptors
in our eyes that detect color called
cones we have cones for red green and
blue and every color we see is a
combination of these

claims.rationale: Standard vision science: humans have three cone classes (L, M, S) with peak sensitivities near red, green, and blue. The one-million-color estimate is a widely cited but approximate figure (actual estimates range from ~100k to several million depending on methodology).

claims.text: A doctor in northern England has four functioning cone types (tetrachromatic vision), allowing her to distinguish up to 100 million colors — roughly 100× more than trichromats.

evidence.quote:
doctor in northern england was found to
have four types of cone cells so her
vision isn't trichromatic it's
tetrachromatic this means she doesn't
just see a million colors she can
distinguish up to a hundred million
different colors

claims.rationale: The 'cDa29' case (Concetta Antico is the famous publicized tetrachromat; a Newcastle study by Gabriele Jordan identified a confirmed functional tetrachromat in the UK around 2010). The 100-million figure is a theoretical upper bound from combinatorial math on 4 vs. 3 cone types — actual measured behavioral discrimination gains are more modest.

claims.text: Approximately 12% of the entire female population should be tetrachromatic based on the genetic statistics of colorblindness in men.

evidence.quote:
around 12
percent of the entire female population
should be tetrachromatic

claims.rationale: This figure is a genetic-carrier estimate — being a genotypic carrier of a fourth cone type does not mean having functional tetrachromatic vision. Jordan et al. (2010) tested 24 carriers and found only one with demonstrable tetrachromat performance. The 12% carrier rate is approximately correct; functional tetrachromacy is far rarer. Video conflates genotype and phenotype.

claims.text: Until a word exists to describe a color, humans have measurable difficulty perceiving and defining it — categorical language shapes perceptual discrimination.

evidence.quote:
most ancient cultures
didn't see blue as a separate color but
rather a shade of green

claims.rationale: This is the video's central thesis — a weak form of the Sapir-Whorf / linguistic relativity hypothesis applied to color. Empirically supported at the categorical-boundary level (Winawer et al. 2007 on Russian blues) but the strong Whorfian claim that one 'cannot see' a color without a word is overstated; perception still operates, categorization is what shifts.

claims.text: Color is not a property of material objects but an interpretation of reflected/absorbed light wavelengths — quoting neuroscientist Beau Lotto at University College London, color is 'a product of light, our culture, our language, and our own imagination'.

evidence.quote:
color is just the way light is absorbed
reflected and scattered by a surface and
if you think of it that way color
doesn't really exist it's just an
interpretation of a wavelength bolado a
neuroscientist at university college
london said there's such a thing as
light there's such a thing as energy
there's no such thing as color color is
nothing more than the product of light

claims.rationale: The host spells the neuroscientist as 'bolado' — the actual UCL researcher is R. Beau Lotto, who has argued exactly this constructivist view of color perception in 'Deviate' and his TED talks. The philosophical claim that 'color doesn't exist' in an object-property sense is consonant with standard vision science; it's framed here as opinion but is mainstream within perception research.
